primary_topics: course structure and selection of YouTube modules
hydrogen porosity in aluminum and titanium welds
GE Lynn titanium compressor electron-beam welding case
titanium metallurgy: alpha/beta phases, alloying elements
Navy titanium alloys (Ti-100, Ti-6Al-2Nb-1Ta) and Sea Cliff / Alvin submersibles
Soviet Alpha-class submarine and Gurevich's titanium welding research
submerged arc welding of titanium (Tom's first MIT contract, late 1970s)
electroslag welding of titanium and Paton Institute heritage
convection patterns in weld pools (Ken Mills hypothesis, Szekely-Eagar 1983 paper)
deep penetration TIG with surface flux
high-power laser physics and stress-relief applications
active armor, kinetic-energy penetrators, MRAP glass armor
key_cases: Engelhard 747-400 titanium ductwork porosity (reagent-grade acetone fix)
GE Lynn $75K titanium compressor parts (free consult, dust contamination)
Caterpillar welder inspection technique (student) and Electric Boat parallel
Sea Cliff and Alvin titanium submersible hulls
Soviet Alpha-class submarine titanium hull construction
Tom's 1979-1980 submerged-arc titanium welding research (Gurevich book, calcium chloride flux)
1980 State Department exchange visit to Paton Institute, Kiev
Iraq War MRAP glass armor development
notable_figures_mentioned: Professor Massa (MIT Ocean Engineering, welding submarines in Japan WWII)
Nick Grant (MIT Materials, NSF exchange program)
John Galata (Tom's first student, Electric Boat)
V.K. Gurevich (Paton Welding Institute, titanium welding metallurgy)
Boris Paton (Paton Institute director, son of E.O. Paton)
E.O. Paton (Hero of the Soviet Union, electroslag welding pioneer)
Ken Mills (UK NPL, weld pool convection)
captioner_quality: poor — heavy garbling of proper nouns and technical terminology throughout; many passages required preservation with editorial flags rather than confident correction
structural_notes: Lecture covers wrap-up of aluminum welding porosity → full treatment of titanium welding → digression into Soviet welding history and Tom's 1979-1980 research → laser physics → military armor applications. Ends mid-thought as Tom goes to fetch a sample for break. No formal Q&A section; student questions interspersed throughout.
